title: Tapping for the Wait-for-Apology Trap
subtitle: A five-minute tapping practice for when you are waiting for someone to apologise before you can move on
excerpt: A five-minute tapping practice for the impasse of waiting for an apology that may never come. Addresses the unfairness of that position and the question of whether your peace has to stay hostage to their acknowledgement.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. The tapping framework is Gary Craig's Emotional Freedom Technique (EFT), in use since the mid-1990s.

practice.whenToUse: Use when you are holding resentment toward someone who has not acknowledged what they did and you are stuck waiting for them to before you can release it.

content.text: A five-minute tapping practice for the position of waiting: waiting for an admission, an acknowledgement, an apology that would allow things to settle. The script addresses what it costs to wait, and whether that cost has to continue.
New to tapping? Read
how EFT tapping works
first.
The practice
Karate chop (repeat x3)
Even though I am still waiting for an apology that may never come, I deeply and completely accept myself.
Even though letting this go feels like letting them off the hook, I deeply and completely accept myself.
Even though I am the one suffering and they have moved on, and that is deeply unfair, I deeply and completely accept myself.
Tapping round
**Eyebrow:** I am still waiting for them to say they were wrong.
**Side of eye:** They may never say it.
**Under eye:** That is deeply unfair.
**Under nose:** My peace is being held by whether they come through.
**Chin:** I am the one stuck. They have moved on.
**Collarbone:** I don't want to let them off the hook.
**Under arm:** But the hook is through me.
**Top of head:** I am more stuck than they are.
Reframe to positive
**Eyebrow:** Releasing this is for me, not for them.
**Side of eye:** What they did was real. My letting go does not change that.
**Under eye:** I can acknowledge what happened and still choose to put it down.
**Under nose:** My peace belongs to me, not to their willingness to apologise.
**Chin:** I can put this down.
**Collarbone:** Releasing it is not saying it was acceptable.
**Under arm:** I am allowed to move forward without their permission.
**Top of head:** My freedom is mine to take back.
Tap each round three times, then move to the reframe and tap that three times.
Where this practice comes from
Original to homemade.education. The tapping framework is Gary Craig's Emotional Freedom Technique (EFT), developed in the mid-1990s from Roger Callahan's Thought Field Therapy.
